meta.title: neo

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Batch 2: transcribed from the owner's roots (Greek and Latin) reference; 2 taught, 2 reach.

root.label: neo
root.mentor_note: neo rarely appears alone; it attaches itself to something old and declares a fresh version of it.

attempt.direction_prompt: A long established idea, or a newly coined one?

context_sentences: The dictionary’s editors add a fresh batch of neologisms every year, most of them born online.
Her lecture opened with a neologism nobody in the room had heard before, and it stuck.
The ward’s quietest corner was reserved for neonates who needed constant, careful watching.
A neonate’s grip, the nurse explained, is stronger than most new parents expect.

parts.gloss: one who grows, a planted one

context_sentences: As a neophyte on the trading floor, he was given the smallest accounts and the longest hours.
The order welcomed each neophyte with a year of quiet, unglamorous training before any real responsibility.

construct_options.text: Someone newly starting out in a subject or role, a beginner
Someone who has just planted a new garden
Someone who has mastered a subject over many years

context_sentences: The old cat’s neophobia meant even a new rug took a week of cautious sniffing before it was trusted.
Some neophobia is healthy in a toddler, though his seemed to extend to every food that wasn’t beige.

construct_options.text: A strong or irrational fear of anything new or unfamiliar
An intense love of new experiences and change
A fear specifically of open or crowded spaces
